confirmation.detail: inbox envelope env_02b10e3d-b6a5-4227-acad-7776d2a1fc63 read back as promoted

payload.local_decision: CPY records the desired runtime posture but defers runtime installation until canonical machinery exists or is explicitly admitted.
payload.problem: Narada can configure and activate the mailbox operation, but there is no clear first-class command that installs a client Site runtime as a separate Windows-startup process with health/read-back evidence, PID/lock semantics, log locations, and uninstall/disable behavior.
requested_capability: Add a canonical command such as narada runtime install-windows-startup --site <site-root> --operation <operation-id> --mode separate-client-runtime --dry-run/--execute.

The command should emit dry-run plan, authority locus, exact Windows startup substrate, service/task name, command line, env/credential binding posture, log paths, PID/health file paths, and uninstall command.
It should distinguish shared User Site runtime from separate client Site runtime.
It should include read-back verification that the startup entry exists and points at the intended Site config.
It should provide status/doctor integration so daemon reality and CLI health agree.
payload.summary: During CPY client-service onboarding, the Operator chose continuous background processing, a separate CPY runtime process, and Windows auto-start. Inspection found no polished Narada CLI command for installing a client Site as its own Windows startup runtime. Staccato precedent shows runtime/daemon observability residuals, so CPY runtime installation was intentionally deferred rather than hand-rolled.
payload.title: Need canonical command for installing client Site as separate Windows startup runtime
why_not_hand_roll: Staccato daemon notes show process reality and CLI health can disagree about daemon running state.
Task Scheduler or Startup-folder installation would create local machinery outside Narada's explicit authority model unless admitted by doctrine and command evidence.
A fresh Operator should not need to know whether to use pnpm daemon, node daemon, scheduled task, Windows service, or shared User Site runtime.
